why-version-control-matters.label: Why Version Control Matters
why-version-control-matters.summary: Open with a standalone story about a project losing its history, then distinguish Git from duplicate final folders and explain the safe local course workspace.

sketch1.title: Worked Example: How Git Solves Project Chaos

sc-1.title: Purpose of Version Control
sc-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a version control system like Git?
sc-1.hint: Think about how version control helps manage changes.

help.concept: Version control systems help manage changes to projects over time.
help.hint_1: Consider how version control tracks changes.
help.hint_2: Version control allows you to revert to previous states.

options.a: To manage changes and track project history
options.c: To increase the speed of development
options.d: To replace the need for project management tools

sc-2.title: Git vs. GitHub
sc-2.prompt: How does Git differ from GitHub?
sc-2.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.

options.a: Git is a local version control system, while GitHub is a hosted platform for collaboration
options.b: Git and GitHub are the same
options.c: GitHub is a version control system, while Git is a platform for collaboration
options.d: Git is used for managing code, while GitHub is used for managing documents

intro-memory.title: The Importance of Project History
intro-memory.bodyMarkdown: Imagine working on a large project with multiple collaborators. Each person saves their work in separate folders labeled 'final', 'final_v2', 'final_v3', and so on. Over time, it becomes nearly impossible to track changes, understand why certain decisions were made, or revert to a previous state if something goes wrong. This is where version control systems like Git come into play. They provide a structured way to manage changes, track history, and collaborate effectively without the chaos of multiple 'final' folders.

worked-example.title: Worked Example: How Git Solves Project Chaos
worked-example.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: Consider a project where a team is developing a new software application. Initially, they save their work in a folder named 'Project'. As changes are made, they create copies like 'Project_final', 'Project_final_v2', and so on. One day, a team member accidentally overwrites a crucial file. Without a version control system, recovering the original file is difficult. However, with Git, every change is recorded as a snapshot. If a mistake occurs, the team can easily revert to a previous version, ensuring no work is lost. This reliable history contrasts sharply with the ambiguous nature of final-copy folder names.
